input.instructions:
Solve this Super-Eywa grading-bank question.
Question ID: coding-B2-usaco-2023-us-open-gold-tree-merging
Title: B2. USACO 2023 US Open Gold / Tree Merging

Problem:
Reconstruct a valid sequence of sibling-merge operations that transforms an initial rooted tree into a final rooted tree.

Grading target:
Binary hidden-test acceptance.

Return format:
FINAL_ANSWER: <your answer>
JUSTIFICATION: <brief justification>

variables.selected_prompt_text:
Write a tentative answer and send one reviewer child a request to perform a step-by-step logical trace of the proposed solution. The reviewer must attempt to find a specific counter-example where the proposed logic fails to reconstruct the correct sequence or violates a problem constraint. If a flaw is found, the reviewer must provide the corrected logical steps required to resolve it.

Return exactly one JSON object in this format:
{
  "schema_name": "eywa_node_response",
  "schema_version": "v1",
  "orchestration_decision": "review",
  "decision_notes": "optional string",
  "draft_response": "plain-text draft answer",
  "message_for_reviewer": "plain-text review request for the child"
}
variables.child_selected_prompt_text:
Solve the question directly.
Keep the entire JSON compact.
Keep decision_notes extremely short. Prefer 3 to 8 words.
Do not narrate your reasoning outside the response field.
In the response field, use exactly two short lines encoded with \n:
FINAL_ANSWER: <answer>
JUSTIFICATION: <brief justification>
Keep the justification to 12 words or fewer.
Use plain text only inside the response field. Do not use markdown formatting, code fences, or math-style backslashes such as \equiv.
Do not add any extra text before or after the JSON object.

Return exactly one JSON object in this format:
{
  "schema_name": "eywa_node_response",
  "schema_version": "v1",
  "orchestration_decision": "execute_locally",
  "decision_notes": "optional string",
  "response": "FINAL_ANSWER: <answer>\nJUSTIFICATION: <brief justification>",
  "result_type": "summary"
}

orchestration.decision_notes: The child result provides the correct algorithmic approach for the tree merging problem. I will format it as requested.

For each final child v, if it represents a merge of initial children c1, c2, ..., ck, record merges (c1, c2), ((c1, c2), c3), ..., in that order. Perform this for all nodes in post-order traversal. Question:
Solve this Super-Eywa grading-bank question.
Question ID: coding-B2-usaco-2023-us-open-gold-tree-merging
Title: B2. USACO 2023 US Open Gold / Tree Merging

Problem:
Reconstruct a valid sequence of sibling-merge operations that transforms an initial rooted tree into a final rooted tree.

Grading target:
Binary hidden-test acceptance.

Return format:
FINAL_ANSWER: <your answer>
JUSTIFICATION: <brief justification>

results.content: FINAL_ANSWER: To reconstruct the sequence of sibling-merge operations, perform a post-order traversal of the final tree. For each node u in the final tree, identify its children v1, v2, ..., vk. For each child vi, determine if it was formed by a merge operation. If a child vi represents a merge of original children c1, c2, ..., cm from the initial tree, record the sequence of binary merges used to create vi: (c1, c2), ((c1, c2), c3), ..., ((...(c1, c2), ...), cm). By processing nodes in post-order, we ensure that all merge operations required to form the subtrees are recorded before the operations required to form the current node u are recorded. This guarantees a valid topological ordering of operations. JUSTIFICATION: A post-order traversal ensures that all descendant nodes (and their corresponding merge operations) are processed and added to the sequence before their parent nodes, satisfying the dependency requirements of the tree structure.
